questions.question_text: Marcus, age 20, has completed 75 hours of pre-license education and passed the broker exam. Can he receive his Illinois broker license?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, at 20 he exceeds the minimum age of 18
distractors: No, he must be at least 21 years old
No, applicants must be 20 with two years of experience
Yes, but only for a residential leasing agent license
questions.explanation: Illinois requires broker license applicants to be at least 18 years old (225 ILCS 454/5-27). The former 21-year minimum was eliminated when the Act was rewritten in 2019, making 'must be 21' a common trap answer. At 20, Marcus qualifies for the broker license; to practice, he must also have a sponsoring broker registered with IDFPR. Managing broker applicants must be at least 20.

questions.question_text: What is the passing threshold for the Illinois broker licensing examination?
questions.correct_answer: Pass each section separately on the 140-question exam
distractors: 70% overall on a combined 100-question exam
80% on each section of a 150-question exam
75% overall on a single 120-question format
questions.explanation: The Illinois broker exam has 140 scored questions, 100 national and 40 state, and each section must be passed separately; passing one section does not offset failing the other. PSI currently reports the passing standard as 70% on the national portion (equivalent to the former scaled score of 75) and 75% (30 of 40) on the state portion.

questions.question_text: Nadia completed her 75-hour Illinois pre-license course 26 months ago but has not yet taken the broker exam. What is her status?
questions.correct_answer: Her coursework has expired and must be retaken
distractors: She may still sit for the exam within 36 months
She can request a six-month extension from IDFPR
Her coursework remains valid indefinitely once completed
questions.explanation: Illinois pre-license education is valid for only 2 years (24 months) after completion. Since 26 months have passed, Nadia's coursework has expired and she must retake the required education before sitting for the exam.

questions.question_text: After passing the Illinois broker exam, what must a new broker do before engaging in licensed activity?
questions.correct_answer: Affiliate with a sponsoring managing broker
distractors: Open a personal escrow account at a local bank
Complete 45 hours of post-license education first
Register independently with the local MLS
questions.explanation: An Illinois broker cannot practice independently. Before engaging in any licensed activity, the broker must affiliate with a sponsoring managing broker who will supervise their work. The license remains inactive until this affiliation is established.

questions.question_text: An Illinois broker license is issued on March 1, 2026. When does this license expire?
questions.correct_answer: It expires after a two-year license term
distractors: It expires after a four-year license term
It expires after a three-year license term
It expires on December 31 of the same year
questions.explanation: Illinois broker licenses operate on a two-year license term. Licensees must complete continuing education and renew before the end of that term to maintain active status.

questions.question_text: A broker with three years of experience wants to become a managing broker. What education must she complete beyond her original pre-license coursework?
distractors: 90 hours of approved continuing education electives
30 post-license plus 30 managing broker course hours
60 hours of advanced brokerage and supervision courses
questions.explanation: To qualify as a managing broker in Illinois, a broker must have held an active broker license for at least 2 consecutive years out of the preceding 3, complete 45 hours of post-license education, and complete an additional 45 hours of managing broker pre-license education (165 total career hours) before sitting for the managing broker exam.

questions.question_text: Sandra holds an Illinois leasing agent license. A property owner asks her to help negotiate the sale of a commercial building. How should Sandra respond?
questions.correct_answer: Decline, as leasing agents cannot negotiate sales
distractors: Proceed only if her managing broker gives written approval
Assist if the building contains fewer than four units
Handle the sale for properties she currently leases
questions.explanation: An Illinois residential leasing agent license is limited to licensed activities related to leasing residential real property (68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.200). Leasing agents may not negotiate sales or work on commercial transactions. Sandra would need a broker license to handle the sale.

questions.question_text: How many hours of pre-license education are required to obtain an Illinois leasing agent license?
distractors: 30 hours
45 hours
75 hours
questions.explanation: Illinois requires only 15 hours of pre-license education for a leasing agent license. This is significantly less than the 75 hours required for a broker license, reflecting the more limited scope of practice for leasing agents.

questions.question_text: An Illinois leasing agent collects a security deposit when a tenant signs a residential lease. What must happen to those funds?
questions.correct_answer: They must be placed in the sponsoring broker's escrow account
distractors: The leasing agent holds them in a personal account until move-in
The leasing agent may retain them as an advance on commission
They must be deposited with IDFPR within five business days
questions.explanation: Security deposits received by a brokerage are escrow moneys under 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.750. Only the sponsoring broker maintains escrow accounts, so a leasing agent who receives a deposit must promptly turn it over for deposit into the sponsoring broker's escrow account, generally no later than the next business day. No licensee may hold client funds personally or commingle them.

questions.question_text: As of 2025, what does the Illinois managing broker exam consist of?
distractors: Both national and state sections with 200 questions
A national section identical to the broker exam
An oral interview plus a written national exam
questions.explanation: As of 2025, the Illinois managing broker exam covers state content only. It does not include a separate national section, since candidates already passed the national portion on their original broker exam.

questions.question_text: A landlord manages and leases units in his own four-unit apartment building without a license. Is this lawful in Illinois?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, owners managing their own property are exempt
distractors: No, any property with four or more units requires a license
Only if he hires a licensed managing broker to oversee leases
Yes, but only for the first 12 months of ownership
questions.explanation: Illinois exempts property owners who manage their own real estate from licensing requirements. This exemption applies regardless of the number of units, as the owner is acting on his own behalf rather than as an agent for others.

questions.question_text: A personal assistant at an Illinois brokerage answers phones and assembles listing packets. The managing broker asks her to show a vacant property to a prospective buyer. What should she do?
questions.correct_answer: Decline, unlicensed assistants cannot show property
distractors: Proceed, since the managing broker authorized the showing
Agree, provided she does not discuss pricing with buyers
Show the property only if a licensed broker is on call
questions.explanation: In Illinois, unlicensed personal assistants may perform clerical tasks such as answering phones, scheduling, and assembling documents. However, they are strictly prohibited from showing property, negotiating terms, or discussing pricing with clients.

questions.question_text: Which professional is exempt from Illinois real estate licensing requirements when performing duties within their own professional scope?
questions.correct_answer: An attorney handling a real estate closing
distractors: A mortgage loan officer listing bank-owned properties
A home inspector negotiating repairs after inspection
An appraiser marketing foreclosure properties for sale
questions.explanation: Illinois exempts licensed attorneys from real estate licensing requirements when they are performing activities that fall within their scope of practice as attorneys. Mortgage officers, inspectors, and appraisers do not receive the same exemption for brokerage activities.

questions.question_text: An unlicensed personal assistant at an Illinois brokerage tells a walk-in client that a listed home is priced below market value and a great deal. What rule has been violated?
questions.correct_answer: Assistants may not give opinions of price or value
distractors: Assistants may not communicate with clients in person
Only managing brokers may comment on property market value
Price opinions require a separate appraisal license in Illinois
questions.explanation: Unlicensed assistants may answer questions only by quoting directly from published information, such as the advertised list price. Interpreting listing information or opining that a home is priced below market value is a licensed activity (68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.740), so the assistant crossed into conduct requiring a license.

questions.question_text: A broker licensed in Indiana wants to practice in Illinois starting in 2026. What process must she follow?
questions.correct_answer: Apply through the new endorsement process
distractors: Transfer her license directly through reciprocity
Retake the full 75-hour Illinois pre-license course only
Obtain a temporary practice permit valid for one year
questions.explanation: Illinois ended reciprocity on January 1, 2026, replacing it with an endorsement process. Out-of-state brokers seeking Illinois licensure now apply through endorsement, providing official certification of their license, passing the Illinois-specific exam portion, and completing a 30-hour endorsement course, rather than relying on the former reciprocity agreements.
